meta.title: DBX-DEA-5: Lakehouse Foundations, Incremental Ingestion, and Workflows

domains.name: ELT with Spark SQL and Python

questions.stem: A team wants to use a storage architecture that keeps open object storage files while still adding table reliability features such as ACID transactions and schema enforcement. Which technology on Databricks best fits that need?

options.text: Parquet files in a plain data lake
Delta Lake tables
CSV files with inferred schema
Managed Hive tables without a transaction log

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Delta Lake layers transaction logs, schema enforcement, and time travel on top of open Parquet files in cloud object storage, satisfying both the openness and reliability requirements.

**A** is incorrect. Plain Parquet files in a data lake provide no ACID transactions or schema enforcement; concurrent writes can corrupt the dataset.

**C** is incorrect. CSV files with inferred schema are fragile — schema changes in source files can silently break downstream reads and there are no transactional guarantees.

**D** is incorrect. Hive tables without a transaction log (non-ACID Hive) do not provide multi-statement atomic commits or reliable concurrent write semantics.

[Delta Lake Introduction](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: Which workspace object is designed to manage reusable code, dashboards, notebooks, and folders for collaborative development?

options.text: Catalog
Workspace
Metastore
Cluster policy

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The Databricks Workspace is the collaborative surface for notebooks, files, dashboards, and folders used in development.

**A** is incorrect. A catalog is a Unity Catalog object that governs data assets such as schemas and tables; it is not a container for notebooks or dashboards.

**C** is incorrect. A metastore is the top-level Unity Catalog container that stores metadata about data objects; it does not hold notebooks or dashboards.

**D** is incorrect. A cluster policy constrains compute settings when users create clusters; it has nothing to do with organizing development artifacts.

[Databricks Workspace](https://docs.databricks.com/workspace/index.html)

questions.stem: A bronze table stores raw JSON from many devices and a silver table standardizes types, removes corrupt rows, and deduplicates records. Which medallion layer is being described by the silver table?

options.text: Landing layer for unchanged source files
Curated business-level aggregates only
Validated and cleaned data ready for downstream modeling
A temporary shuffle spill layer created by Spark during joins

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. The silver layer is the validated and conformed zone: types are standardized, corrupt records are removed, and duplicates are resolved before data is used for downstream analytics or gold-layer aggregates.

**A** is incorrect. The bronze (or landing) layer stores raw, unchanged data exactly as it arrived from the source. The silver layer transforms it.

**B** is incorrect. Curated business-level aggregates describe the gold layer, not silver. Silver is about data quality and conformance, not business-ready summaries.

**D** is incorrect. Shuffle spill is an internal Spark execution concept related to memory management during operations like joins or sorts; it is not a medallion architecture layer.

[Databricks Lakehouse Architecture](https://docs.databricks.com/lakehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: Which statement about Delta Lake time travel is correct?

options.text: It requires copying old versions into a separate history database
It lets users query prior table versions or timestamps while retention permits
It works only for streaming tables, not batch tables
It replaces the need for checkpoints in Structured Streaming

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Delta Lake time travel allows reads of earlier table versions or timestamps using `VERSION AS OF` or `TIMESTAMP AS OF` syntax, as long as retained history files have not been vacuumed away.

**A** is incorrect. Time travel does not require copying data into a separate history database. Delta Lake uses its transaction log and retained Parquet files to reconstruct earlier states in place.

**C** is incorrect. Time travel works for all Delta tables regardless of whether they were written by a streaming or batch job.

**D** is incorrect. Structured Streaming checkpoints track streaming progress and state; they serve a completely different purpose from time travel and are not replaced by it.

[Delta Lake Table History](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/history.html)

questions.stem: A new engineer needs a SQL endpoint for BI dashboards without managing Spark clusters directly. Which compute option is the best fit?

options.text: Serverless or classic SQL warehouse
Single-user job cluster
All-purpose cluster with ML runtime
Delta Sharing recipient profile

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SQL warehouses (serverless or classic) are purpose-built for SQL analytics and BI dashboard workloads. They expose a SQL endpoint and abstract cluster management from the user.

**B** is incorrect. A single-user job cluster is designed for automated pipeline runs, not interactive SQL querying for dashboards. It does not expose a SQL endpoint.

**C** is incorrect. An all-purpose cluster with the ML runtime is optimized for iterative notebook-based machine learning, not for serving BI dashboards via SQL.

**D** is incorrect. A Delta Sharing recipient profile is a credential object used to authenticate data consumers in a cross-organization sharing scenario; it is not a compute resource.

[Databricks SQL Warehouses](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/admin/sql-endpoints.html)

questions.stem: Which statement about Photon is most accurate for the associate exam scope?

options.text: Photon is a governance feature in Unity Catalog
Photon is the vector search engine for GenAI workloads
Photon is a high-performance query engine that can accelerate SQL and DataFrame workloads
Photon is the notebook version-control service

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Photon is Databricks' native vectorized query engine written in C++ that accelerates many SQL and DataFrame operations on clusters where it is enabled.

**A** is incorrect. Unity Catalog handles governance; Photon is an execution engine optimization, not a governance feature.

**B** is incorrect. Mosaic AI Vector Search is the feature for GenAI vector retrieval; Photon is unrelated to embedding search.

**D** is incorrect. Version control for notebooks is handled by Databricks Repos (Git integration), not Photon.

[Photon Overview](https://docs.databricks.com/runtime/photon.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to share a Delta table with another organization without copying the data into the recipient account. Which feature should they consider first?

options.text: Delta Sharing
Auto Loader
Databricks Connect
MLflow Registry

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Delta Sharing is an open protocol for secure, zero-copy sharing of data across platforms and organizations. The data stays in the provider's storage; recipients read it via a secure endpoint without needing a copy.

**B** is incorrect. Auto Loader is a Databricks file ingestion tool for loading files from cloud storage into Delta tables; it does not facilitate cross-organization sharing.

**C** is incorrect. Databricks Connect allows developers to run Spark computations from a remote IDE against a Databricks cluster; it is a development connectivity tool, not a data sharing protocol.

**D** is incorrect. MLflow Registry manages machine learning model versions and their lifecycle; it is not used for sharing Delta tables with external organizations.

[Delta Sharing](https://docs.databricks.com/delta-sharing/index.html)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes schema evolution in Delta Lake?

options.text: It rejects any write where the source has extra columns unless explicitly enabled
It automatically changes partition values for old rows when new columns are added
It can add compatible columns when explicitly enabled during writes or merges
It converts all columns to STRING to avoid type-mismatch failures

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Schema evolution in Delta Lake allows compatible changes — such as adding new columns — when enabled via `mergeSchema` option or `MERGE` schema evolution settings. It does not happen automatically without opt-in.

**A** is incorrect. While Delta does reject extra columns by default, option C more precisely and completely describes the full schema evolution behavior, including the ability to allow changes.

**B** is incorrect. Schema evolution does not rewrite partition values for existing rows. Adding a new column leaves null values in that column for historical rows.

**D** is incorrect. Delta Lake does not cast all columns to STRING. Type mismatches that cannot be automatically widened result in write failures rather than silent coercion to string.

[Update table schema | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/update-schema.html)

questions.stem: What is the main reason a data engineer would partition a large Delta table by a frequently filtered date column?

options.text: To guarantee zero-cost cloud storage for the table
To reduce data scanned for queries that filter on that column
To disable the Delta transaction log for that column
To prevent the need for any future OPTIMIZE or ZORDER operations

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Partitioning by a column allows Spark to skip entire partition directories when a query includes a filter on that column, reducing the amount of data scanned and improving query performance.

**A** is incorrect. Partitioning has no impact on cloud storage costs per byte. It can sometimes increase the number of small files, which may slightly increase metadata overhead.

**C** is incorrect. The Delta transaction log always exists and is not affected by partitioning choices.

**D** is incorrect. Partitioning does not eliminate the need for OPTIMIZE or ZORDER. Small-file problems within partitions are common and OPTIMIZE is often still needed.

[Delta Lake Partitioning](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-batch.html#partitioned-writes)

questions.stem: Which statement best contrasts a lakehouse with a traditional data lake?

options.text: A lakehouse cannot support machine learning workloads alongside SQL analytics
A lakehouse stores only unstructured data and prohibits structured tables
A lakehouse requires data to be moved into a proprietary closed format
A lakehouse adds table management and reliability features on top of object storage

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. The lakehouse model retains the openness and low cost of object storage while adding data management capabilities such as ACID transactions, schema enforcement, governance, and performance optimizations — things a raw data lake lacks.

**B** is incorrect. A lakehouse supports structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data. Restricting to unstructured data only describes a file-storage layer, not a lakehouse.

**C** is incorrect. Lakehouses use open file formats (typically Parquet) rather than proprietary closed formats. This openness is a defining characteristic.

**A** is incorrect. A key advantage of the lakehouse is that it supports both SQL analytics and machine learning workloads on the same data, eliminating the need for separate systems.

[Databricks Lakehouse](https://docs.databricks.com/lakehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: A PySpark transformation must return all rows where `status` equals `ACTIVE` and `country` equals `US`. Which expression is valid?

options.text: `df.filter(col("status") == "ACTIVE" and col("country") == "US")`
`df.filter((col("status") == "ACTIVE") & (col("country") == "US"))`
`df.where("status" = "ACTIVE" and "country" = "US")`
`df.select(status == ACTIVE, country == US)`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. PySpark Column objects must be combined using bitwise operators (`&` for AND, `|` for OR) with each condition wrapped in parentheses. This is the valid and idiomatic PySpark syntax.

**A** is incorrect. Python's `and` keyword does not work on PySpark Column objects — it evaluates the truthiness of the Column object itself and raises a `ValueError` at runtime, not a compile-time error.

**C** is incorrect. The string SQL expression form uses `==` not `=` for equality comparison, and the quoted strings `"status"` and `"country"` are treated as literals, not column references. This syntax is invalid.

**D** is incorrect. `df.select` projects columns and cannot filter rows. Additionally, `status == ACTIVE` without quotes references undefined Python variables.

[PySpark API reference | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/pyspark/pyspark-api.html)

questions.stem: Which SQL function is most appropriate for turning an `ARRAY<STRING>` into multiple rows, one element per row?

options.text: `explode`
`collect_list`
`coalesce`
`flatten`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `explode` takes each element of an array and generates one output row per element, effectively turning an array column into multiple rows.

**B** is incorrect. `collect_list` is an aggregate function that goes in the opposite direction — it collects values from multiple rows into a single array.

**C** is incorrect. `coalesce` returns the first non-null value among a list of expressions; it does not transform arrays into rows.

**D** is incorrect. `flatten` removes one level of nesting from an `ARRAY<ARRAY<T>>` to produce a single `ARRAY<T>` — it does not produce multiple rows from an array.

[Spark SQL Functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/functions/explode.html)

questions.stem: A pipeline must combine daily sales tables from two regions that have identical schemas. Which operation is most appropriate?

options.text: Inner join on a shared key
Left anti join
Cross join
Union

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. A union (or `UNION ALL`) appends rows from two datasets that share the same schema, which is exactly what's needed when stacking regional sales tables.

**A** is incorrect. An inner join combines columns from two tables based on matching keys, producing one merged row per match. It is used when you need to relate rows across tables, not stack rows from tables with the same schema.

**C** is incorrect. A cross join produces the Cartesian product of two tables — every row from the left paired with every row from the right. This would generate incorrect and explosively large results for regional sales data.

**B** is incorrect. A left anti join returns only rows from the left table that have no match in the right table. This would find region-A records absent from region-B, not combine both regions.

[Set operators | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-qry-select-setops.html)

questions.stem: Which statement about user-defined functions on Databricks is most accurate for basic ETL work?

options.text: UDFs should usually be preferred over built-in functions because they always optimize better
Built-in Spark SQL functions are generally preferred when they can express the logic
Python UDFs can only be written in Scala, not Python
UDFs automatically make all transformations run in a vectorized manner

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Built-in Spark SQL functions are executed natively within the JVM and benefit from Catalyst optimizer integration and Photon acceleration. They should be used whenever they can express the required logic.

**A** is incorrect. UDFs typically perform worse than built-in functions because Python UDFs incur serialization overhead and bypass the Catalyst optimizer. They should be used only when no built-in equivalent exists.

**C** is incorrect. Databricks supports UDFs in Python, Scala, Java, and SQL. Python UDFs (`spark.udf.register`) are very common in practice.

**D** is incorrect. Standard Python UDFs process one row at a time, not in a vectorized manner. Pandas UDFs (vectorized UDFs) must be explicitly defined to achieve vectorization, and even then they do not make all transformations vectorized automatically.

[Databricks UDFs](https://docs.databricks.com/udf/index.html)

questions.stem: A SQL query needs to replace `NULL` values in `discount_pct` with 0. Which expression is simplest?

options.text: `discount_pct OR 0`
`NVL(discount_pct, 0)`
`SUM(discount_pct, 0)`
`CAST(discount_pct AS DECIMAL(10,2)) DEFAULT 0`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `NVL(expr, replacement)` (equivalent to `COALESCE(expr, replacement)`) returns the first non-null argument. When `discount_pct` is NULL it returns 0, and when it is not NULL it returns the original value.

**A** is incorrect. `discount_pct OR 0` is a boolean logical expression, not a null-substitution function. It does not replace NULLs with 0 and would produce unexpected results or errors depending on the column type.

**C** is incorrect. `SUM` is an aggregate function that sums values across rows; it cannot be used as a scalar null-replacement expression with two arguments.

**D** is incorrect. `CAST` converts data types but `DEFAULT 0` is not valid syntax in a SELECT expression. This would result in a parse error.

[Spark SQL NVL Function](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/functions/nvl.html)

questions.stem: Which join returns only rows from the left table that do not have a matching key in the right table?

options.text: Left semi join
Left anti join
Full outer join
Cross join

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. A left anti join returns all rows from the left table for which there is no matching row in the right table — the exact inverse of a left semi join. This is commonly used to find records in one table that are absent from another.

**A** is incorrect. A left semi join returns rows from the left table that do have a matching key in the right table (the existence check), which is the opposite of what is needed here.

**C** is incorrect. A full outer join returns all rows from both tables, matching where possible and filling with NULLs where there is no match. It includes rows from both sides, not just unmatched left rows.

**D** is incorrect. A cross join produces the Cartesian product of both tables with no join predicate; it does not filter based on key matching or non-matching.

[Spark SQL JOIN Types](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-qry-select-join.html)

questions.stem: A table contains one row per order and a nested struct column `shipping_address` with a sub-field `city`. Which syntax selects the city in Spark SQL?

options.text: `shipping_address[city]`
`shipping_address:city`
`shipping_address.city`
`GET_PATH(shipping_address, 'city')`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Dot notation is the standard Spark SQL syntax for accessing a named field within a struct column. `shipping_address.city` retrieves the `city` sub-field from the `shipping_address` struct.

**A** is incorrect. Bracket notation with a string key (`column[key]`) is used to access elements of a MAP by key, not fields of a struct. For struct field access, dot notation is required.

**B** is incorrect. The colon syntax `column:path` is the Databricks-specific shorthand for extracting fields from a VARIANT or semi-structured JSON column, not for typed struct fields.

**D** is incorrect. `GET_PATH` is not a standard Spark SQL function for struct access. It is used in other SQL dialects (e.g., Snowflake) for semi-structured data traversal.

[Spark SQL Struct Fields](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-datatypes.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer must compute total revenue by customer. Which clause groups rows before aggregation?

options.text: `ORDER BY customer_id`
`DISTRIBUTE BY customer_id`
`GROUP BY customer_id`
`HAVING customer_id IS NOT NULL`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `GROUP BY customer_id` partitions rows into groups by the specified column so that aggregate functions like `SUM(revenue)` operate on each group independently.

**A** is incorrect. `ORDER BY` sorts the result set after all grouping and aggregation have been performed. It does not group rows for aggregation.

**B** is incorrect. `DISTRIBUTE BY` controls how rows are distributed across partitions for a query's shuffle operations; it is used for output partitioning in Hive-style queries, not for defining aggregation groups.

**D** is incorrect. `HAVING` filters groups after aggregation has been applied. It is used with `GROUP BY` to eliminate groups based on aggregate conditions, but it does not define the grouping itself.

[Spark SQL GROUP BY](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-qry-select-groupby.html)

questions.stem: Which higher-order function is best for keeping only positive elements from an integer array column?

options.text: `transform`
`filter`
`flatten`
`aggregate`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The `filter` higher-order function applies a predicate lambda to each array element and returns a new array containing only elements for which the predicate returns true — ideal for keeping only positive values.

**A** is incorrect. `transform` applies a lambda to each array element and returns a new array of the same length with transformed values. It maps every element; it cannot remove elements.

**C** is incorrect. `flatten` removes one level of nesting from an array-of-arrays, producing a single flat array. It does not filter elements based on a predicate.

**D** is incorrect. `aggregate` reduces an array to a single scalar value by iteratively applying a merge function (similar to `reduce`). It produces a scalar result, not a filtered array.

[Spark SQL Higher-Order Functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/functions/filter.html)

questions.stem: A source column contains timestamps as strings like `2026-06-01 14:30:00`. Which function should a data engineer use to convert them to timestamp values?

options.text: `date_trunc`
`to_binary`
`substring_index`
`to_timestamp`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. `to_timestamp(string, format)` parses a string representation of a datetime into a Spark timestamp type. It supports optional format strings for non-standard layouts.

**B** is incorrect. `to_binary` converts a string to binary/byte array encoding (Base64, hex, etc.); it does not parse datetime strings.

**C** is incorrect. `substring_index` extracts a substring up to a given delimiter occurrence; it performs string manipulation, not datetime conversion.

**A** is incorrect. `date_trunc` truncates a timestamp to a specified precision (year, month, day, hour, etc.); it operates on values already of timestamp type and does not parse strings.

[Spark SQL to_timestamp](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/functions/to_timestamp.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that correctly describe a Delta `MERGE INTO` operation. (Select TWO)

options.text: It can update matched target rows based on source data
It can insert new rows when no target match exists
It always requires the target table to be partitioned by the merge key
It can be used only from Python notebooks, not from SQL

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `MERGE INTO` supports a `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE` clause that updates target rows whose key matches a source row.

**B** is correct. `MERGE INTO` supports a `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT` clause that inserts source rows that have no matching target row, enabling upsert patterns.

**C** is incorrect. `MERGE INTO` does not require the target table to be partitioned by the merge key. It works on any Delta table regardless of partitioning, though partitioning on the key can improve performance.

**D** is incorrect. `MERGE INTO` is a SQL statement and can be executed in Databricks SQL notebooks, SQL cells, or via the SQL API. It is also available through the Delta Lake programmatic API in Python and Scala.

[Delta Lake MERGE INTO](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-merge-into.html)

questions.stem: Which command most directly registers a DataFrame as a temporary view for SQL queries in the same Spark session?

options.text: `createOrReplaceTempView`
`saveAsTable`
`cacheTable`
`broadcast`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `df.createOrReplaceTempView('view_name')` registers the DataFrame as a session-scoped temporary view that can be queried with SQL using `spark.sql('SELECT * FROM view_name')`.

**B** is incorrect. `saveAsTable` writes the DataFrame to a permanent managed or external Delta table in the metastore. It persists beyond the session and is not a temporary view.

**C** is incorrect. `cacheTable` caches a table already registered in the metastore into Spark's memory for faster repeated queries. It does not register a new temporary view from a DataFrame.

**D** is incorrect. `broadcast` is a hint function used to tell Spark to broadcast a small DataFrame to all executors during a join, reducing shuffle. It does not create a queryable view.

[CREATE VIEW | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-view.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to write a DataFrame to an existing Delta table while preserving old rows and adding only new rows. Which write mode should they use?

options.text: overwrite
errorifexists
ignore
append

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Append mode adds the DataFrame's rows to the existing table without removing any prior data — exactly the requirement to preserve old rows while adding new ones.

**A** is incorrect. Overwrite mode replaces all existing data in the table with the new DataFrame. This would delete old rows, which violates the stated requirement.

**C** is incorrect. Ignore mode silently skips the write if the target table already exists and leaves it unchanged. Since the table already exists, no new rows would be added.

**B** is incorrect. ErrorIfExists mode raises an exception if the target table already exists, preventing the write entirely rather than appending new data.

[Delta Lake Write Modes](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-batch.html#append)

questions.stem: When should a data engineer most likely use a window function instead of a simple `GROUP BY`?

options.text: When the result should preserve individual rows while also computing values like rank or running totals
When all duplicate rows must be permanently removed from a table
When the goal is to convert a wide table into a normalized long format
When joining two tables on a composite key

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Window functions (e.g., `ROW_NUMBER()`, `RANK()`, `SUM() OVER (...)`) compute analytics over a defined window of rows while retaining one output row per input row. `GROUP BY` collapses rows into one per group, losing row-level detail.

**B** is incorrect. Removing duplicate rows is typically done with `SELECT DISTINCT`, `QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() = 1`, or `MERGE` deduplication — not with window functions as the primary tool.

**C** is incorrect. Converting a wide table to a long format uses the `UNPIVOT` operation (or `stack` function in Spark). Window functions do not reshape table structure.

**D** is incorrect. Joining tables on a composite key is accomplished with a `JOIN ... ON col1 = col2 AND col3 = col4` clause. Window functions are unrelated to join operations.

[Window functions | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-window-functions.html)

questions.stem: A JSON payload includes many optional fields and occasionally adds new keys. Which file format and table strategy on Databricks is generally the strongest fit for reliable incremental analytics?

options.text: Raw text files queried directly with no schema
CSV files loaded into temporary views only, refreshed each run
Delta tables populated after parsing the JSON, with schema evolution enabled
ORC files written to a folder without a transaction log

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Parsing the JSON into Delta tables provides schema enforcement, schema evolution for new keys, ACID transactions, and efficient incremental reads via streaming or Change Data Feed — the strongest foundation for reliable incremental analytics.

**A** is incorrect. Querying raw text files with no schema provides no type safety, makes filtering and aggregation cumbersome, and offers no incremental processing capability.

**B** is incorrect. Temporary views built from CSV files are session-scoped, not durable, and do not support efficient incremental processing. They rebuild from scratch each time, which is inefficient for analytics pipelines.

**D** is incorrect. ORC files without a transaction log share many of the same weaknesses as plain Parquet — no ACID guarantees, no schema evolution support, and no built-in incremental read mechanism.

[Delta Lake Overview](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: A team ingests files that arrive continuously in cloud storage and wants scalable file discovery, schema inference, and checkpointed incremental processing. Which Databricks feature should they choose first?

options.text: Auto Loader
Delta Sharing
Databricks SQL Dashboards
COPY INTO with a scheduled batch job

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Auto Loader is designed specifically for incremental file ingestion from cloud storage. It supports scalable file discovery using directory listing or file notification events, automatic schema inference and evolution, and Structured Streaming checkpoints for exactly-once processing.

**B** is incorrect. Delta Sharing is used to securely share Delta tables with other organizations or teams. It does not ingest files from cloud storage.

**C** is incorrect. Databricks SQL Dashboards are a visualization and reporting tool. They query existing tables; they do not provide file ingestion or incremental processing capabilities.

**D** is incorrect. `COPY INTO` is suitable for simpler bounded file ingestion scenarios. For large-scale continuous arrival, Auto Loader scales better with its file notification mode and streaming checkpoint support.

[Databricks Auto Loader](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem: Why is a checkpoint location important in Structured Streaming?

options.text: It stores BI dashboard screenshots for historical comparison
It tracks streaming progress and state so the query can recover correctly after a failure
It forces every micro-batch to overwrite the target table
It is used only for batch jobs that read from Delta tables

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Checkpointing in Structured Streaming writes source offsets, committed offsets, and operator state to durable storage after each micro-batch. On restart after a failure, the engine reads the checkpoint to resume from where it left off and avoid reprocessing or skipping data.

**A** is incorrect. Checkpoints store streaming metadata and state — not BI screenshots or visualization artifacts.

**C** is incorrect. Output mode (append, update, complete) determines how data is written to the sink, not the checkpoint. A checkpoint does not change the write behavior to overwrite.

**D** is incorrect. Checkpoints are a core concept of Structured Streaming and apply only to streaming queries. Batch jobs do not use streaming checkpoints.

[Structured Streaming Checkpoints](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/checkpoints.html)

questions.stem: Which Structured Streaming output mode emits only the rows that were updated or newly created in the latest micro-batch, for aggregate queries that support it?

options.text: update
complete
append
overwrite

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `update` output mode emits only the rows that changed in the current micro-batch. For a running count aggregation, for example, only the groups whose counts changed are written to the sink.

**B** is incorrect. `complete` output mode re-emits the entire result table on every micro-batch, regardless of which rows changed. This is more expensive but required for some sinks that cannot handle row-level updates.

**C** is incorrect. `append` output mode adds only new rows to the sink and never updates existing rows. It is valid only when output rows cannot change after being emitted (e.g., non-windowed aggregations with no late data).

**D** is incorrect. `overwrite` is a batch write mode, not a Structured Streaming output mode. Structured Streaming uses `append`, `update`, and `complete`.

[Structured Streaming Output Modes](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/output-mode.html)

questions.stem: A streaming source may deliver late events. What is the main purpose of a watermark on event time?

options.text: To encrypt records older than a specified threshold
To define how long the engine should wait for late data before finalizing and trimming aggregation state
To cast event-time timestamp columns to a date type
To partition the output table by event time automatically

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. A watermark specifies the maximum expected lateness of events. The engine keeps aggregation state for a window until the watermark has passed that window's end time, after which late events for that window are dropped and state is trimmed to bound memory usage.

**A** is incorrect. Watermarks have no effect on data encryption. They are purely a streaming progress and state-management concept.

**C** is incorrect. Watermarks do not perform type conversion. The event-time column is already expected to be a timestamp; the watermark uses it to track lateness.

**D** is incorrect. Watermarks do not automatically partition output. Output partitioning is controlled by the write configuration, not the watermark definition.

[Structured Streaming Watermarks](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/watermarks.html)

questions.stem: Which statement best compares `COPY INTO` and Auto Loader?

options.text: `COPY INTO` is often convenient for idempotent SQL-based loading of a bounded set of files, while Auto Loader is stronger for ongoing scalable ingestion
Auto Loader can load only CSV, while `COPY INTO` supports all formats
`COPY INTO` is required for all Delta Live Tables pipelines
Auto Loader processes files in reverse chronological order to prioritize recent data

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `COPY INTO` is a SQL command well-suited for idempotent, repeatable loads of a known set of files (e.g., daily batch loads). Auto Loader is a Structured Streaming source built for large-scale continuous ingestion with efficient file discovery, schema inference, and checkpoint-based exactly-once semantics.

**B** is incorrect. Auto Loader supports many formats including JSON, CSV, Parquet, Avro, ORC, text, and binary. The format restriction described does not apply.

**C** is incorrect. Delta Live Tables pipelines can use Auto Loader as a source; they are not required to use `COPY INTO`. DLT supports multiple ingestion patterns.

**D** is incorrect. Auto Loader processes files in discovery order (based on cloud storage listing or notification events), not reverse chronological order.

[Auto Loader vs COPY INTO](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem: A team reads from Kafka in Structured Streaming and wants to write exactly one output record per unique event id. Which approach is most directly aligned with that goal?

options.text: Convert the stream to a pandas DataFrame before writing to avoid Spark write duplication
Run `VACUUM` before every micro-batch to remove duplicate files
Disable checkpoints to avoid reprocessing events after a failure
Use `dropDuplicates` with the event id, usually together with event-time watermarking when needed

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. `dropDuplicates(subset=['event_id'])` in Structured Streaming removes duplicate events by tracking seen IDs in state. When combined with a watermark, state is bounded and old event IDs are eventually evicted from memory.

**B** is incorrect. `VACUUM` removes old data files from Delta tables that are no longer referenced by the transaction log. It is a maintenance operation for storage reclamation, not a deduplication tool for streaming.

**C** is incorrect. Disabling checkpoints is counterproductive for deduplication. Without checkpoints, the engine cannot track which events have been processed, making reprocessing after failure more likely to produce duplicates, not fewer.

**A** is incorrect. Converting a stream to pandas does not deduplicate by event id across micro-batches. Pandas operates on a single batch in memory and has no awareness of previously seen events across batches.

[What is stateful streaming? | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/structured-streaming/stateful-streaming.html)

questions.stem: What is a micro-batch in Structured Streaming?

options.text: A small Delta table that is cached permanently in Spark memory
A small increment of new data processed as one unit during a streaming trigger
A cluster type reserved for low-latency notebook execution
A Unity Catalog feature for tracking data lineage at the row level

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Structured Streaming's micro-batch execution model collects new data that has arrived since the last trigger into a small batch and processes it as a single Spark job, then repeats on the next trigger interval.

**A** is incorrect. A permanently cached Delta table is a Spark cache or a DLT materialized view — not a micro-batch. Micro-batches are transient units of data processing, not stored objects.

**C** is incorrect. There is no cluster type called a "micro-batch cluster". All-purpose and job clusters both support streaming; the cluster type is unrelated to the micro-batch concept.

**D** is incorrect. Unity Catalog provides data lineage tracking at the table and column level, not at the row level. Micro-batch is a streaming execution concept, unrelated to Unity Catalog features.

[Structured Streaming Programming Guide](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/index.html)

questions.stem: A source system lands daily full snapshots, but the downstream silver table should contain only the latest version of each business key. Which technique is most appropriate?

options.text: Use `MERGE INTO` on the business key to upsert each snapshot into the silver table
Use `UNION ALL` to append every snapshot and query the latest using a window function each time
Write every snapshot to a separate dashboard filter panel
Disable schema enforcement on the silver table to absorb all snapshot variations

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `MERGE INTO` keyed on the business identifier upserts each daily snapshot: existing records are updated to reflect the latest state and new keys are inserted. The silver table always reflects the current version of each record.

**B** is incorrect. Appending all snapshots with `UNION ALL` causes the table to grow without bound and requires a deduplication step on every read using a window function. This is inefficient for a silver table meant to hold only the latest record per key.

**C** is incorrect. Dashboard filter panels are a presentation-layer concept in BI tools, not a data storage or ETL technique. Snapshots cannot be routed to them.

**D** is incorrect. Disabling schema enforcement does not address the requirement of maintaining only the latest record per business key. It would allow corrupt or type-mismatched data to enter the silver table silently.

[Delta Lake MERGE INTO](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-merge-into.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO characteristics of Auto Loader schema evolution support. (Select TWO)

options.text: It can rescue unexpected fields into a rescued data column for later inspection
It can detect and propagate schema changes for newly arriving files when configured with schema hints
It requires the engineer to manually list every new file path in the source option
It stores streaming progress only inside BI dashboard metadata tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Auto Loader's rescued data column (`_rescued_data`) captures fields that do not match the inferred or provided schema, preserving them for downstream inspection instead of dropping or failing on them.

**B** is correct. When schema evolution mode is enabled, Auto Loader can detect new columns or type changes in arriving files and evolve the inferred schema over time without manual intervention.

**C** is incorrect. Auto Loader automatically discovers files using either directory listing or cloud storage event notifications. Engineers do not need to maintain a manual list of file paths.

**D** is incorrect. Streaming progress (offsets and metadata) is stored in the checkpoint directory specified via `checkpointLocation`. BI dashboard metadata tables are entirely unrelated to streaming progress tracking.

[Auto Loader Schema Inference and Evolution](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/schema.html)
questions.reference: [Configure schema inference and evolution in Auto Loader](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/schema.html)

questions.stem: A streaming query reads from a Delta table and writes to another Delta table. Which capability allows efficient incremental downstream processing from table changes?

options.text: Change data feed enabled on the source Delta table
Notebook widget parameters bound to the source table
Databricks SQL alert thresholds on the source table
Cluster tags applied to the compute running the query

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Delta Change Data Feed (CDF), when enabled on a table, records row-level insert, update, and delete changes. Downstream consumers can read only the changed rows rather than re-scanning the full table, making incremental processing highly efficient.

**B** is incorrect. Notebook widget parameters are interactive UI inputs that allow users to parameterize notebook code. They do not expose table changes for downstream streaming.

**C** is incorrect. Databricks SQL alerts send notifications when a query result crosses a threshold. They do not provide a mechanism for downstream jobs to incrementally consume changed rows from a table.

**D** is incorrect. Cluster tags are metadata labels applied to compute resources for cost attribution and governance purposes. They have no effect on how data changes are exposed or consumed.

[Delta Change Data Feed](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-change-data-feed.html)

questions.stem: Which Databricks feature is used to orchestrate scheduled notebook, Python, SQL, and JAR tasks as dependent jobs?

options.text: Databricks Workflows
Unity Catalog
Photon
Databricks Repos

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Databricks Workflows orchestrates scheduled and dependency-aware jobs. Each job can contain multiple tasks (notebook, Python script, SQL, JAR, Delta Live Tables pipeline) with configurable dependencies forming a DAG.

**B** is incorrect. Unity Catalog manages data governance — catalogs, schemas, tables, and access controls. It does not schedule or orchestrate compute jobs.

**C** is incorrect. Photon is a vectorized query execution engine that accelerates SQL and DataFrame operations. It is not an orchestration or scheduling feature.

**D** is incorrect. Databricks Repos integrates workspaces with Git repositories for version control of notebooks and files. It does not schedule or orchestrate job execution.

[Databricks Workflows](https://docs.databricks.com/workflows/index.html)

questions.stem: A workflow contains several tasks where task C should run only if both A and B succeed. What should the engineer configure?

options.text: A cluster policy that limits C to run after a timeout
Task dependencies for C that require tasks A and B to succeed first, in the workflow DAG
A metastore assignment to enforce task execution order
A Delta Sharing recipient grant on task C's output

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. In Databricks Workflows, task dependencies express execution order and success prerequisites. Setting task C to depend on tasks A and B ensures C only starts after both A and B have completed successfully.

**A** is incorrect. Cluster policies define compute constraints (instance types, auto-scaling limits, etc.) for cluster creation. They have no concept of task execution ordering or dependencies.

**C** is incorrect. A metastore assignment determines which Unity Catalog metastore is attached to a workspace for data governance. It is not a mechanism for controlling job task execution order.

**D** is incorrect. Delta Sharing recipients and grants control data access for external consumers. They have no relationship to workflow task dependencies or execution sequencing.

[Databricks Workflow Task Dependencies](https://docs.databricks.com/workflows/jobs/jobs.html)

questions.stem: What is a key advantage of using a job cluster instead of an always-running all-purpose cluster for scheduled ETL?

options.text: Job clusters reduce idle cost because they are created for each run and terminated when the run completes
Job clusters bypass Unity Catalog access controls for faster data access
Job clusters support more Spark versions than all-purpose clusters
Job clusters allow multiple users to share a notebook session simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Job clusters are ephemeral: they start when a job run begins and terminate when it ends. This eliminates the cost of a cluster sitting idle between job runs, which is a common inefficiency with always-on all-purpose clusters used for scheduled ETL.

**B** is incorrect. Job clusters are subject to the same Unity Catalog access controls as all-purpose clusters. Security policies apply regardless of cluster type.

**C** is incorrect. Both job clusters and all-purpose clusters support the same Databricks Runtime versions. The cluster type does not determine which runtimes are available.

**D** is incorrect. Job clusters run a single job with isolated compute. All-purpose clusters are the ones shared across multiple users for interactive notebook work.

[Classic compute overview | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/use-compute.html)

questions.stem: A production workflow should retry a flaky API ingestion task automatically before the whole pipeline is marked failed. Which job setting is most relevant?

options.text: Dashboard refresh interval on the output dashboard
Cluster Spark version selection
Notebook markdown comment in the first cell
Task retry policy with a maximum number of retries and optional delay between attempts

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Databricks Workflows allows configuring a retry policy per task, specifying the maximum number of retries and an optional delay between attempts. This handles transient failures in flaky tasks like external API calls without failing the entire job immediately.

**B** is incorrect. The Spark version (Databricks Runtime version) controls which Spark and library versions run on the cluster. It has no effect on retry behavior for task failures.

**C** is incorrect. Markdown comments in a notebook are documentation annotations. They do not influence job execution, error handling, or retry behavior.

**A** is incorrect. A dashboard refresh interval controls how often a Databricks SQL dashboard re-executes its queries. It is unrelated to job task retry policies.

[Databricks Job Task Retries](https://docs.databricks.com/workflows/jobs/jobs.html)

questions.stem: Which statement about Delta Live Tables (or Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines) is most accurate at a high level?

options.text: They are designed to help define managed, declarative data pipelines with built-in expectations and operational features
They replace all need for SQL warehouses and eliminate interactive querying
They are designed exclusively for serving real-time inference from ML models
They disable column-level lineage tracking to improve pipeline throughput

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Delta Live Tables (DLT) / Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines provide a declarative framework where engineers define tables and transformations; the platform manages orchestration, dependency resolution, data quality expectations, and operational monitoring.

**B** is incorrect. DLT pipelines are for data engineering transformations. SQL warehouses are for interactive SQL analytics and BI queries. The two serve different use cases and do not replace each other.

**C** is incorrect. DLT is a data pipeline framework for ELT transformations. Serving real-time model inference uses Model Serving (Mosaic AI Model Serving), a completely separate capability.

**D** is incorrect. DLT enhances lineage tracking by recording table dependencies within the pipeline. It does not disable lineage features.

[Delta Live Tables Overview](https://docs.databricks.com/delta-live-tables/index.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to promote notebook code through pull requests and keep changes synchronized with a remote Git provider. Which Databricks capability helps most directly?

options.text: Databricks Repos
Feature Store
Serverless SQL warehouses
Mosaic AI Vector Search

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Databricks Repos integrates workspace notebooks and files with remote Git repositories (GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Bitbucket). Engineers can commit changes, create branches, and open pull requests directly from the workspace.

**B** is incorrect. Feature Store manages machine learning features and their metadata for model training and serving. It does not provide Git integration or source control capabilities.

**C** is incorrect. Serverless SQL warehouses provide on-demand SQL compute for analytics queries. They are unrelated to source control or code promotion workflows.

**D** is incorrect. Mosaic AI Vector Search provides vector similarity search for GenAI and RAG applications. It is an AI/ML feature with no connection to Git-based code management.

[Databricks Repos](https://docs.databricks.com/repos/index.html)

questions.stem: Which result best indicates a healthy bronze-to-silver production pattern?

options.text: Raw source files are edited in place after every downstream validation error
Gold tables are used only for storing raw, unchanged source payloads as the landing zone
The silver layer applies validation, type standardization, and deduplication before trusted downstream use
Every pipeline task writes results directly to BI dashboard tables instead of Delta tables

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. In a healthy medallion architecture, the silver layer transforms bronze data by validating records, enforcing types, and removing duplicates, producing a trusted dataset ready for gold-layer aggregates or direct analytics use.

**A** is incorrect. Editing raw source files in place violates the bronze layer's role as an immutable raw archive. Errors should be addressed downstream, not by modifying the raw data.

**B** is incorrect. The gold layer is for curated, business-level aggregates — not a raw data landing zone. Bronze is the landing layer for raw, unchanged source data.

**D** is incorrect. Writing pipeline results directly to dashboard storage rather than Delta tables bypasses the reliability benefits of the medallion architecture and eliminates the ability to reprocess or audit data.

[Databricks Medallion Architecture](https://docs.databricks.com/lakehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, which securable is the top container in the three-level namespace hierarchy that then contains schemas?

options.text: Table
Catalog
Volume
Storage credential

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: catalog → schema (database) → table/view/volume. The catalog is the top-level container and the first segment of a fully qualified name such as `catalog.schema.table`.

**A** is incorrect. A table is a leaf-level securable within a schema. It is the third level of the hierarchy, not the top container.

**C** is incorrect. A volume is a Unity Catalog securable for managing files in cloud storage within a schema. It lives inside a schema (level 2), not at the top of the hierarchy.

**D** is incorrect. A storage credential is a Unity Catalog securable that holds the cloud credential used to access a storage path. It is a workspace-level object, not part of the catalog → schema → table hierarchy.

[Unity Catalog Object Model](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A user can query a table only if they have the required privileges through a direct grant or a group membership. Which statement about explicit deny rules in Unity Catalog is correct?

options.text: Unity Catalog supports a `DENY` privilege that overrides any existing `GRANT`
Unity Catalog uses grant-based authorization only; there is no explicit `DENY` privilege
`DENY` exists but applies only to external locations and storage credentials
`DENY` is required before `REVOKE` can remove a privilege in Unity Catalog

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Unity Catalog authorization is purely grant-based. Access is allowed only when the user or one of their groups has been explicitly granted the required privilege. There is no `DENY` statement — removing access is done with `REVOKE`.

**A** is incorrect. Unity Catalog does not implement a `DENY` privilege. Traditional SQL `DENY` semantics (overriding grants) are not part of Unity Catalog's permission model.

**C** is incorrect. There is no `DENY` privilege in Unity Catalog for any securable type, including external locations and storage credentials. Access to those objects is also grant-based.

**D** is incorrect. `REVOKE` removes a previously granted privilege directly. No `DENY` statement is required or exists as a prerequisite for `REVOKE` in Unity Catalog.

[Unity Catalog Privileges](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to control which cloud storage paths can be used for external tables and managed ingestion in Unity Catalog. Which securable is designed for that purpose?

options.text: External location
SQL warehouse
Dashboard folder
Auto Loader checkpoint directory

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. An external location in Unity Catalog binds a storage credential (which holds the cloud IAM permissions) to a specific cloud storage path. Granting `READ FILES` or `WRITE FILES` on an external location controls who can access data at that path.

**B** is incorrect. A SQL warehouse is a compute resource for running SQL queries. It does not govern access to cloud storage paths.

**C** is incorrect. Dashboard folders organize Databricks SQL dashboards. They have no role in controlling access to cloud storage paths for external tables or ingestion.

**D** is incorrect. An Auto Loader checkpoint directory is a path used by a streaming job to store progress metadata. It is not a Unity Catalog securable and does not govern which storage paths are accessible for external tables.

[Unity Catalog External Locations](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-external-locations-and-credentials.html)
questions.reference: [Connect to cloud object storage using Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-external-locations-and-credentials.html)
